source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 7.3.1-7.3.2 useradd (pp. 104-107)

questions.q: Create the user carine with UID 1000, primary group GID 1013, and home directory /home/GroupC/carine.
questions.why: -u sets the UID, -g the primary group, -d the home directory; the login name comes last.

questions.q: Create the user albert with primary group GroupA and supplementary groups GroupP and GroupC.
questions.why: -g sets the primary group; -G takes a comma-separated list of supplementary groups.

questions.q: What is the state of a freshly created account (useradd with no password step)?
options: It has no password and is locked; the user must be given a password to unlock it
It is immediately usable with an empty password
It is enabled with a default password of 'password'
It is deleted after 24 hours if no password is set
questions.why: At creation the account has no password and is locked (shadow shows !!); it stays unusable until a password is assigned.

questions.q: With no options, what does useradd set up by default for the new user?
options: A home directory and a primary group both named after the user, and the /bin/bash shell, with an auto-assigned UID/GID
No home directory, and the /sbin/nologin shell
A shared /home directory and GID 0
A random shell chosen from /etc/shells
questions.why: Defaults: a same-named home directory and primary group, /bin/bash, and a UID/GID auto-deduced (usually 1000-60000), read from /etc/login.defs and /etc/default/useradd.

questions.q: What does the -M option of useradd do?
options: Creates the user WITHOUT a home directory
Creates a system account
Forces creation of the home directory
Adds the user to the wheel group
questions.why: -M skips creating the home directory (whereas Debian's useradd needs -m to force one).

questions.q: When useradd creates the home directory, what does it copy into it?
options: The template files from /etc/skel (e.g. .bashrc, .bash_profile, .bash_logout)
The contents of /root
The whole of /etc
Nothing - the directory is left empty
questions.why: useradd populates the new home directory with the skeleton files from /etc/skel.

questions.q: Set the DEFAULT primary group to 1000, base home directory to /home and default shell to /bin/bash for future user creation (edit /etc/default/useradd via useradd).
questions.why: useradd -D sets the defaults in /etc/default/useradd; -g default group, -b base directory, -s default shell.

questions.q: What is the maximum length of a Linux user name, per the guide's naming rules?
options: 32 characters
8 characters
16 characters
There is no limit
questions.why: User names may be up to 32 characters, using lowercase letters, numbers and underscores; avoid starting with a number and don't clash with an existing group.
